text: Marcelo was simply far too offensive, even before Brazil completely lost it. Germany's right side is probably the strongest part, with Lahm and Müller on the wing. Having a super offensive wing back on that side immediately carved Brazil wide open.
label: analysis

text: In a situation where you have a wing back high up the field, you either want your winger drop back immediately or you want your defensive CM to fill in the gap. With Khedira, Schweinsteiger and Kroos in the midfield, Luiz Gustavo dropping back meant huge amounts of space there.
label: analysis

text: The 4-0 came after few passes after kickoff. The ball goes to the CBs of Brazil, they pass to Fernandinho, Kroos pressures, Fernandinho fails to hold the ball, two quick passes to Khedira and back, done deed.
label: analysis

text: Brazil frequently overlapped the German defense with long balls, something that Germany had already displayed vulnerability against in the France match. They even tried to exploit the space behind Lahm when he ventured upfield.
label: analysis

text: Lahm plays to Müller, who jogged back a few meters. Hulk covers Schweinsteiger, Oscar covers Kroos. Luis Gustavo is out of position at left back together with Marcelo, because he had to move there during the counter attack. Fernandinho is in front of the center backs. Alone.
label: analysis

text: Due to all the defenders being bound by their respective duties or out of position, no one can meet Müller's run from inside the penalty area. Kroos plays to Müller — Dante isn't able to intercept. Klose is completely free from the left. Finishes. Scores.
label: analysis

text: Brazil seemed to try and play the same game they always played: very aggressive pressing high up, relying on tackles rather than doubling or blocking passes. Then lots of quick long balls to Hulk/Fred/Bernard who stayed high up and wide. It left way too much space to exploit in midfield. They had 10 tackles compared to 28/31/18/18/16 in previous games.
label: analysis

text: Thiago Silva has helped cover for the backline in two ways. First, as the best defender of the group, he's made a lot of key tackles and interceptions when other players were out of position. Second, his vocal leadership seems to have gone a long way towards making sure other defenders, Luiz particularly, stay at home rather than going forward.
label: analysis

text: Germany let their fullbacks and midfielders come as far forward as they liked — look at where Khedira, nominally one of the two holding midfielders, ended up on all the German goals. Lahm also. And look at how completely clueless the Brazilian defense were at dealing with these runs from deep.
label: analysis

text: This Brazilian defensive unit for the past year or two has almost always played against teams who are terrified of leaving Neymar space on the counterattack and therefore only attack with four or five players at a time. Brazil seemed completely baffled by Khedira coming forward — that's a player who in their other games hasn't attacked.
label: analysis

text: The space that Germany had on the second goal when the game is still in doubt was astounding. Kroos is given acres of time at the top of the box when Fernandinho misses the interception and Dante doesn't close him down. Marcelo tracks Müller but never gets closer than 3 yards. Maicon is caught ball watching and fails to put a body on Klose.
label: analysis

text: Scolari chose Bernard rather than Ramires to replace Neymar — this clearly showed he was going all or nothing, choosing a pacey out-and-out winger to attack rather than someone more defensive who might have helped contain that packed, technical German midfield.
label: analysis

text: Brazil's whole plan in attack was to get the ball to Neymar. With him gone the attack had to find chemistry and understanding that they weren't forced to have before. David Luiz needs to constantly be told where to be or else he ends up ball-chasing around the entire pitch. You lose Thiago Silva and suddenly Luiz is the one organizing the defense.
label: analysis

text: Fernandinho was involved in all 4 of the opening goals. Where Brazil fell apart was in the triangle Dante-Luiz-Fernandinho. Luiz did not step up to be the leader he needed to be, Fernandinho kept making bad decisions, and Dante stood there wondering what Luiz and Fernandinho were doing.
label: analysis

text: Marcelo was way too far down his flank on the second goal — he needs to be much higher up making sure the pass goes into the box. Luiz, Dante and Maicon are way too far down in their own box. Maicon does some real lazy defending, pushes off Klose and doesn't close him down.
label: analysis

text: Brazil played attacking football with a lot of overlapping from wingbacks and committed a lot of players to attack. That works if you can retrieve very quickly when you lose it — good high pressing is almost a necessity. Their pressing was terrible, almost nonexistent, and therefore they were very exposed on counterattacks.
label: analysis

text: Worst thing to happen to Brazil was winning the Confederations Cup last year. Felipão and players got confident — guys like Robinho and Luis Fabiano were not pushing for a place, and guys like Dani Alves and Fred were barely playing anything the last twelve months because they were already picked.
label: analysis

text: Lucas Moura's omission from the team was the real headscratcher — he's the closest resemblance to Neymar Brazil have. With him in the side they could have plugged and played their normal style. I was also baffled why Willian was left on the bench in favor of Bernard.
label: analysis

text: David Luiz has played very well for Chelsea as a defensive midfielder through making strong tackles in front of the defense and moving forward when he finds space. Brazil hasn't even explored this option in the countless friendlies leading up to the tournament. There seemed to be no Plan B.

text: Understanding the defensive side of the game is incredibly hard. There are no nice statistics around it — nobody can yet quantify how positioning stopped an opportunity being created 15 seconds later, or drove the attacking side into making decisions that blocked a goalscoring chance. You would end up analysing counterfactuals with tens of different what-ifs every second back you analyse.
label: analysis

text: There is a reason the highest paid positions in both NFL and football are the more attacking positions — their contributions are most easily analysed and have numbers put on them. It's easiest for fans to understand they scored the goal. A great goalkeeper may have no saves but still have made a huge impact in stopping multiple goals through denying the shot chance, but there is no nice way to quantify this yet.
label: analysis

text: Van der Sar went from being named top goalkeeper in Europe after winning the CL with Ajax, to utter shambles playing for Juve. He was out to sea playing behind a deep, very Italian defensive line — he couldn't read the game how he normally did playing with such a high line at Ajax. Wasn't until Fergie got his hands on him, 6 years after leaving Ajax, that his quality was brought out once again.
label: analysis

text: The Jabulani's unpredictability was from not having enough drag — it was too smooth. When kicked it did not experience a lot of drag so the typical Magnus effect would not take place. With little spin it was not stabilising in an expected curve, opening it up to very random movement when it slowed down during its flight and experienced more drag.
label: analysis

text: The Trionda and Jabulani were both based on tetrahedrons — most balls are based on icosahedrons — leading to more roundness. The Jabulani's unpredictability was from too much drag, so they put divots on the Trionda to reduce it.
label: analysis
notes: Border case first sentence is factually wrong (Jabulani had too little drag per other comment), but structured technical argument; labeled analysis on form

text: The Trionda was released last October. Chances are they at least played with the same mold of ball during international windows and friendlies — in Spain, the Copa del Rey final was played with that most recent design. Unless the balls were completely flawed in production, they're not going to change the cup model, so why not do your homework.
label: analysis

text: Played keeper at the college level. All three of these made me scratch my head when I saw them live. Good positioning, well timed save attempt, hands on the ball, shot placement isn't great — these are damn near routine saves. World class keepers know the pace of the ball off the foot intuitively and these types of shots just don't sneak up on you. It has to be the ball.
label: analysis

text: I bought one and have hit a good few hundred shots with it. The thing moves and bends all over the place. The corners have been spectacular but some wingers do seem to be struggling with longer crosses curving too much.
label: analysis

text: Played as a keeper to a fairly decent level. He gets his hand to it well in time on the Pickford save — it looked so odd because that's a textbook save any keeper should make. People who haven't played football, especially in goal, have the oddest perception of what's par for the course for keepers versus what's exceptional.
label: analysis

text: In the NFL, cornerbacks and D-line are among the highest paid positions now because outsize talent in those areas really changes how a defense has to give space. RBs are not paid well anymore because the drop off from great to good isn't that much — most of the value of a run is from great blocking.
label: analysis

text: xG started as simple as more shots = more goals, then started taking into account positions on the pitch. Then it moved to taking into account context — counterattack, slow developed play, positions of defenders. Now it is starting to take into account the key players, so Haaland from the exact same position will have a higher xG than someone who can't kick a ball reliably.
label: analysis

text: The defense in front of a keeper changes what's required of them, and in turn changes how well they perform. The keepers who went from godly to garbage and vice versa seemingly overnight is the best illustration — Van der Sar is the best example.
label: analysis

text: This is why ex-keepers on comms are underrated. A slow-mo freeze frame makes every shot look saveable — someone who can talk through the flight, body shape and footwork is much more useful than just saying 'should do better there'.
label: analysis

text: Its relevant to have the ball debate at every World Cup because these balls aren't used before the tournament and you really only have a few games to judge. The Trionda's tetrahedron panel shape makes it swerve and respond to drag in ways icosahedron-based balls don't.
label: analysis

text: Hart is a consummate professional when it comes to goalkeeping analysis — it's a brilliant mix of relevant knowledge that comes with top level experience and active thought from an outside perspective. The best punditry comes from this kind of position-specific analysis, because no one person can perfectly break down a whole game when they can't have top level experience in every position.
label: analysis

text: Hart's analysis on keeping is first rate, as is Michael Owen's about strikers and finishing. In Owen's case, it's when he analyses other aspects of the game that's the problem.
label: hot_take

text: Hart is fucking unreal when it comes to goalkeeping analysis. Instead we get Carragher, Neville and Keane spouting off shit for hot takes.
